text: hello and welcome respected chairpersons and delegates i will be presenting ventral mesh rectopexy for recurrent rectal prolapse this is a sixty seven year old lady who complains of mass descending per rectum for six months which could be reduced manually she doesn't give history of constipation though she is suffering from depression for last ten years she also underwent rectal prolapse surgery twice once in the form of thiers procedure followed by and delorme's procedure one year back On examination, per abdomen is soft. Per rectally, the previously polypropylene sutures of Delorme's repair could be palpated.

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position with laparoscopic ports placed. A polyamide suture on a straight needle is used to elevate the uterus, enhancing visualization of the deep cul-de-sac. The rectum is elevated for the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory and makes an incision over the peritoneum at this location. The peritoneum is divided, allowing entry into the retrorectal space and presacral plane, which is visually noted as an avascular area. A monopolar device is used to perform a hemostatic dissection in this plane.
The surgeon places an inverted J-shaped incision on the right side of the peritoneum, marking the incision site and tattooing the J portion for clear identification.

The surgeon begins anterior dissection by lifting and dividing the rectovaginal septum using two instruments for traction. Monopolar energy is applied to separate the septum, removing the surrounding fat and exposing the bare anterior surface of the rectum.

The surgeon continues the division of the rectovaginal septum, extending the dissection up to the level of the perineal body. The assistant confirms the positions of the vagina and rectum by palpation, while any remaining ligaments and attachments are divided to fully mobilize the prolapsed rectum.
The surgeon continues posterior dissection up to the pelvic floor, preserving the lateral ligament on the right side. The dissection halts upon reaching the pelvic floor.

Now a measurement of the length of mesh required is taken using an umbilical cotton tape. This is a representative video from another surgery because this part of the suturing was not captured in the present case.

The surgeon uses Ethibond IIO sutures to attach the mesh to the anterior surface of the rectum. The mesh is then secured to the sacral promontory, ensuring the rectum is stabilized to prevent prolapse. Two sutures are placed, and the mesh is further fixed along the mesentery of the rectum.

The surgeon applies fibrin glue to fix the mesh securely to the anterior wall of the rectum. Subsequently, the peritoneum is closed using a V-lock running suture to extraperitonealize the mesh, marking the conclusion of the rectopexy procedure.
